data.text:
INT: Tape 22 of Mark Verstandig. Mark, would you like to introduce your family, please? 

MV: Yes, thanks. 

INT: First the young lady next to you on your left. 

MV: Yeah, the young lady next to me on my left, that is my wife Frieda. And me her husband, at least until 120. 

And this is my grandson Aron. 

FRIEDA VERSTANDIG: The youngest one 

MV: The youngest. And this is my daughter, Felicity, my David, the grandson. He's the middle one. And behind me is Sydney. He's my son-in-law. 

INT: And who is your eldest grandchild? 

MV: My eldest grandchild, Leah, is at this very moment in Israel. After the matriculation, she went for a year to Israel. 

INT: And the other young lady on your left, behind your wife is Felicity. 

FELICITY BLOCH: Yeah, I was introduced already. 

MV: That is Felicity. This is my daughter, Felicity. 

FELICITY BLOCH: 

INT: Felicity, would you like to say something about how your parents' experiences have shaped your thinking, in particular, the way you helped your dad put his book out. 

FELICITY BLOCH: Well, I'm glad you gave me an opportunity for a plug. So-- 

--this book is now going to be published next month, again, by Melbourne University Press. And it's been quite a remarkable experience. It came about almost by accident. I really had no sense that this would take the shape it has. But my father began writing, and the manuscript just developed. 

He wrote it in Yiddish, again, almost by accident. It was almost a trick to get him to write it. And then I found myself in the position of having to translate it. And the process of translation was very powerful, because it was so slow. 

Everything that I read had to be digested and absorbed and pondered to get it right into English. And that made it sink in. And I felt-- I became very much more intimate with my parents. And at the same time, it opened up to me a world that I had known very little about because very little was said about the past and about the families that had disappeared or the way of life. 

And it wasn't all tragic, either. It was just fascinating to me to learn about the way of life before the war as well as the horrible experiences they had during the war. And almost incidentally, I think, I'm also got a great feeling for the language, which is in the process of disappearing. I felt this is a great treasure of humor and wisdom and a strong expression, which needs, in some way, to get recognition. So I hope that the translation has helped that. 

INT: Books such as your father's, of course, are immensely important, because they preserve the experience of a very unfortunate generation, as you might say, or groups of people who went through the Holocaust, most of whom did not return to tell their tale. Why do you think it's important that your father has written this book and, again, recorded his experiences on film? 

FELICITY BLOCH: Well, I think, obviously, this-- we're going through a very strange period in history. There was a period of silence and repression, where very little was said about the past. And then there's this period of remembering, where we are realizing that the last witnesses won't be with us for very long. And we need to know their story. And there is a kind of a desperate need to know and to find out as much as we can before it's too late. 

And we have been just overwhelmed by the depth of the response to this book since we've published it, partly from people who have got a stake in it already, the Jewish community, who feel that their way of life is getting recognition, their stories, somehow, are being mirrored in this. But also from the non-Jewish community, from people who have approached us, just off the street, in the post office, the people who come up to my father and say they've read this book. They find it absolutely wonderful. They really knew nothing about what happened. 

And I think part of is that the book is so detailed, that, like a novel, it actually creates a world so that you have almost-- it's hard to say it, but it's almost like the pleasure of the old kind of fiction of the 19th century novel, which creates a world for you that you can live in and experience for yourself. So that's-- that's, I think, been part of the-- the value of the book, the recognition that it's had. 

INT: Finally, what have you been able, and what do you think others of your generation can learn from the experiences of those people fortunate enough to survive the Holocaust? 

FELICITY BLOCH: Well, I think you take nothing for granted. You don't feel too complacent about living in a country like Australia. I-- we're, in the moment, in the middle of this 'stolen generation' scandal, the story of the aboriginal children who were taken away from their parents to be fostered. They were dumped in institutions. They were brutally treated. And I can't believe that this was going on when I was an adolescent and a student, and I knew nothing about it. 

And yet in somehow-- somehow I'm responsible. I'm part of that society that allowed that to happen. And at the same time, there is a terrible refusal to acknowledge the suffering of those people. And, you know, the political repercussions at the moment are causing a lot of anguish to a lot of people in Australia. And I think you find a lot of Holocaust survivors are standing up and saying-- finding some sort of element of recognition in what's happening-- what happened in Australia, that it's not just something that happened out there in another world. It could happen here. It did happen here. 

I couldn't believe a story last week, that there were mass graves, in country towns, in Australia, of children who had been murdered or died of neglect or illness, mass unmarked graves in Australian institutions in suburbs and country towns. This was just something I thought would have only happened, you know, in some distant past, you know, that you can call the Holocaust. But it's-- it's not something you can ever forget about. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This-- 

INT: Who are these people, Mr. Verstandig? 

MV: On the left is my mother. Her name was-- was Gela, Gela Verstandig. On the right is my father, Lajzer Verstandig. The picture is-- was made certainly before the turn of the century. 

INT: In which town? 

MV: It could have been Sokolow. It could have been Mielec. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This-- 

INT: Who are these three dashing young men? 

MV: Yes, on the left is my father. The middle is my father's brother, Adolf. And on the right is his other brother, Leon. My father was the oldest one. Adolf was the youngest one. And Leon was in the middle-- was in the middle between them. This picture was taken in Sweden. It could have been about 1905 or something like that-- '05 or '08. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Before the war. This is my older sister, Gizella. Picture was made in Sweden, before the world war, before the First World War. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Should I-- 

Yes, and this is my brother, Solomon. The picture was made, maybe, in 19-- during the war, maybe in Bratislava, in [NON-ENGLISH]. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Yes, this is-- 

INT: This-- this photo is? This photo is your sister? 

MV: Yes, this is my sister Rucha. This picture was made in Mielec in 1926. 

INT: OK, thank you. 

MV: You can see the school uniform. This is my sister [NON-ENGLISH], Berta. This picture was made in Mielec in 1929. 

This is my sister, Sucha. The picture was made in [NON-ENGLISH] in 1936. 

Yes, this is my family from Sweden. But the picture was made in Cracow, in Cracow about 1923. On the right side is Paulina, my auntie. And next to her is Gina, her daughter. And to the left is Solomon, the oldest child of my-- of this family. 

We're coming down. On the right is Monish, or Marcus. And in the middle is Sigmund. And to the left is Philip. Yeah, they and-- from all those people, Sigmund is alive in Sweden. And Philip is alive in Sweden. 

INT: Who is this group of young people? 

MV: This is-- I will-- I will enumerate them. On the left is Leah, Leah Ostrow, my cousin. Next to her is my other Solomon. Behind him is myself. To the right is a girl called-- I think her name was Mindla Garfunkel. 

And then there was my sister, Sucha, a friend of the family, Aron Sempler. And to the right is my sister, Rucha. This picture was made in July 1931 on the lawn in Sadkowa-Gora. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: I don't remember the name. I can't recall the name of this man on the left. Then there is Ita and Solomon. That's my sister-in-law with my brother and Willie, their son. Later, we have got three children. One is Lana, and on the other, on the right-hand side, is Hala. They were the children of my sister Golda Reches and Moshiska. In the middle is Louise, my niece. This is the daughter of my brother Solomon and Ita. Louise is now-- lives now in Melbourne. 

INT: And that photo was taken in which year? 

MV: This photo must be taken in 1938. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This is my brother, Solo and his wife Ita after their wedding in 1931. 

This is Sabina [NON-ENGLISH], Frieda's older sister, who died of leukemia in 1937. An enlargement from her graduation photo, at the-- at the teacher training college, taken in about 1921. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This picture-- on the left-hand side is Dr. Jacob Schalpeter, and then his wife, Lola, and Dr. Shimon Mala. The picture was made in Krynica, a Carpathian resort, taken in 1934, '25, a year after the Schalpeter's marriage. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: That is Frieda's sister, Lola [NON-ENGLISH], a matriculation photo taken in 1927, '28. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Yeah, that is the Mielec shul. On the 13th September 1939, many Jews were burnt alive in this shul. The shul was bulldozed after the war. 

That is Mielec shul after it was burned down. 

That this Felicity in front of our manor house in Sadkowa-Gora. The picture was taken in 1990. 

Yeah, that is Mielec's Gymnasium. The picture was taken after the war. 

That is the Mielec's courthouse. Mielec's [NON-ENGLISH], the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] live. 

Yes, that is our house. That was our house at the main square, the [NON-ENGLISH] in Mielec, a picture taken after the war. 

Yes, that is Frieda's-- Frieda's house. That was the house of Frieda's parents and of her uncle, together. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Yes, that was our academic house, where we lived where-- while I was a student, a Jewish house. During the war, the Germans turned it into a brothel. And today, it is a student-- this is-- this is a music students' house without any indication that it once belonged to the Jews. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This is a picture taken during the deportation of the Jews from Mielec. A number of similar-- of-- of pictures, showing the deportation will appear in the next edition of the book-- 

INT: And how did you-- 

MV: I Rest My Case. 

INT: And how did you obtain these photos? 

MV: These photos were made by a photographer called Jaderny. He was a very good photographer, a very nice man. And he had a sense of his-- I mean, he-- he realized that what he is doing, what-- photographing it all will-- will-- will have the value of documents. 

INT: And how did you get these photos later on? 

MV: Yeah, there is today the Jaderny-- Jaderny museum in Mielec. And there are many pictures of different-- from different periods-- from different periods, made by him. And they are part-- and these pictures are-- are displayed over there in this museum. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This is another picture of the deportation of Jews in Mielec, made by Jaderny. I don't recognized the people. 

This is a picture of my cousin, Leah Hal and her daughter, Lana. Photo dated Mielec, 8 October, 1942, just before Leah went into hiding. Probably the last time she saw her child. Leah was murdered by Polish Partisans on the 31st of May, 1944. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Yes, that is my picture, taken after liberation in August 1944. It was when I needed a new identity card after the liberation. And with that picture, I went up to the city council, and the clerk over there was a school friend of mine, a school mate of mine. And when I came up, he asked me who authorized me to take down this band with the-- with the Star of David, which we had to wear when-- during the German occupation. 

So I explained to him that the-- the Germans had no right to issue such a decree. It was against the international law, against the constitution, and that the Germans are no more here. I don't have to wear it. 

And he said to me, you have to wear it until our government will see it fit to change the law to allow you not to wear-- not to wear this arm band. And I told him, look. I didn't come here to listen to your stupid things. Either you give me my identity card, or I'll bring here a-- a-- a-- a Soviet officer up. His name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And you'll tell him to put on a-- to-- to-- to put on a Star of David. 

And he was scared. And he gave me the identity card. And in the mean-- in-- in the end, I did him a favor, because if I would have listened to him and put on the band and went down, and the town was swarming with Russian officers and Jewish officers and soldiers, because this was the front line. He would have been in Siberia. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: That shows you only the attitude of the Poles. 

Yeah, this photo shows Cheslow and Batalia Kubick our friends from Polaniec. And they helped us during the war. I wrote about them in my book. Today, they live in Lutsk. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: That picture was taken in 1950. Stefania and Franciszek Markowski in Chrzastow. They-- they-- they sheltered us during the war, before the liberation. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: This is Marcin Valas, who-- who was leading us from one hiding place to another hiding place, a trusted man. 

This is Dr. Kasprowicz, the man who risked his life coming through she-- to-- to-- to-- to Frieda after she was wounded. And he examined her. 

Yeah, this is a photograph of of [NON-ENGLISH] and Jan [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] not far from Polaniec. Who-- he-- they gave us shelter for about three, four months. 

That is Cheslow Kubick, how he looked in 1990. And to the left is [NON-ENGLISH], the daughter of the family [NON-ENGLISH] Who provided us with shelter. And that is Kubick, who brought us, in December 1943, to the [NON-ENGLISH], to-- to-- to this hiding place. 

On the left, that's myself. Next to me is Frieda. Then is Louise, the daughter of my brother and his Ita. That is Ita Verstandig, Willie Verstandig, their son, Solo, and the younger brother of Ita, Bernard Foster. The picture was taken in 1946 before we left Poland. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: Yeah, this is a photograph of an ID issued by the Federation of Polish Jewry, in Germany, in the American occupation zone in Germany. They were the publishers of the Ibergang Weekly, where I was one of the editors of this paper. 

This is an ID card issued by Unsere Welt. That is-- that was a weekly-- a weekly in the German occupation zone. And I was the political editor of this paper. 

This is the front page of an issue, Ibergang. It's written, [NON-ENGLISH]. It means it is a-- it is a paper of the Polish Federation in Germany. 

An article in-- signed by me, written by me, for the Ibergang, where I was one of the editors. Editor-in-chief was Libhaber, there. Besides this, there were three other editors, and we made the paper. It was a weekly paper. 

Yes, this is a commentary. It's-- it's in Yiddish. The Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. That is a commentary on the restitution laws in Poland, which I published-- which was published by the Federation of the Polish Jewry in Germany. 

INT: And who is this handsome young couple? 

MV: That was made in Germany, 1946, showing myself and my wife, Frieda. We looked a little younger than today. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: A picture made in 1947, showing myself and my wife, Frieda, in Berchtesgaden, not far from Hitler's-- from-- from Hitler's residence when he was still alive. 

INT: Thank you. 

MV: He never expected such guests over there. 

Picture showing Frieda in the old school in Paris, training as a machinist. 

The Verstandig family on board the Sylvania, January 1952. From left to right, Mark, Frieda, Felicity, Ita, Solo, Louise, and Willie. 

This is my granddaughter, Leah. She matriculated last-- end of the last year, and received the-- the Premier's award for literature in 1996. And she will spend in Israel a year and will be back by the end of 1997.
